objections.objection: The scenario's survival hinges on the unstated claim that teacher supply and readiness for multigrade pedagogy are adequate without systemic reform. This directly contradicts the scenario's own cited evidence: half of Hungarian teachers are 50+, rural vacancy postings are rising ~25% year-on-year, and Finnish evidence shows initial teacher-education programmes do not prepare multigrade teachers. The concrete flaw: assuming scalable multigrade quality can be achieved through ad hoc in‑service training and local goodwill alone ignores demographic pipeline shortfalls and the long lead-time for preservice curriculum change, so the model collapses if teachers cannot be recruited or upskilled at scale.
objections.suggested_revision: Make teacher-supply realism explicit: commission a district-level audit of multigrade-qualified teacher supply and retirement/entry projections through 2030; cost and fund a multi-year preservice+in‑service multigrade certification pathway with binding staffing targets per tankerület; include funded rural recruitment incentives and a contingency plan (temporary visiting‑specialist pools or shared specialist posts) if pipeline audits show shortfalls.

objections.objection: The scenario assumes existing commuting infrastructure and informal transport arrangements can be scaled to safely carry upper-primary pupils daily to central ISCED2 sites. This ignores concrete operational evidence in the dossier: regional buses sometimes refuse unaccompanied minors and parents currently organize escort shifts—signs of an already strained system. The concrete flaw: centralization collapses if reliable, supervised daily transport (including unaccompanied-minor policy compliance, supervision, and acceptable journey times) cannot be guaranteed, because unsafe/unstable transport will produce welfare harms, dropout, or parental resistance that undermine the reform's educational gains.
objections.suggested_revision: Replace the unsupported transport assumption with prerequirements: mandate a transport-safety and capacity audit (route-level) before any centralization decision; pilot centralized upper-grade transfer in 2–4 regions with funded, supervised bus services (including escorts where needed) and measured welfare/attendance outcomes; only scale national restructuring after pilots demonstrate safe, costed transport solutions and acceptable pupil travel times.

objections.objection: S3 assumes Polish-style association-run and inter-municipal network models are legally and operationally transferable to Hungary despite the dossier's own note that post-2013 centralization removed municipal maintainer authority. The concrete flaw: treating Poland's gmina-based legal mechanism as plug-and-play ignores that Hungarian tankerület centralization removes the local legal locus for maintainer transfer; without legal pathway and protections against political reversion, association-run models cannot function sustainably and so the scenario collapses if maintainership cannot be legally delegated or protected.
objections.suggested_revision: Do not assume transferability; commission a formal Hungarian-Polish legal-comparative analysis to map required amendments to Nkt. and maintainer law, design statutory safeguards (e.g., multi-year funding contracts, non-revocable maintainer status clauses for community associations), and run time-limited pilot association-run schools under temporary regulatory waivers plus state capacity-building grants to test operational sustainability before broader rollout.

objections.objection: The scenario's core depends on sustained political will and cross-administration funding to build and maintain a national registry, thresholds, and training standardization. The concrete flaw: the dossier itself documents the absence of cross-party consensus and politically contingent reversals (e.g., maintainer seizures), so the assumption that a non-binding framework will be funded and used neutrally is unsupported; without statutory ring-fencing and institutional safeguards the framework will be undercut or selectively instrumentalized, nullifying its intended transparency and equity benefits.
objections.suggested_revision: Convert the advisory design into a durable institutional package: specify statutory ring-fenced multi-year funding, create an independent academy or watchdog (with cross-party representation and legal mandate) to host the registry, and build binding rules for how thresholds must be used in tankerület decisions (e.g., mandatory publication, mandatory independent review when thresholds are breached). Pilot the registry with legislated minimum data-disclosure standards and an automatic sunset-review clause linked to parliamentary oversight to reduce the risk of political erosion.
